para.text: For each conviction of a chargeable offense listed in §§C—I of this regulation, the Department shall enter on the record of the person the number of points listed for that particular conviction.
The points associated with each conviction are described in §§C—I of this regulation. All statutory references are to Natural Resources Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, unless otherwise noted.
Tier I. The following violations are Tier I violations. A person who receives a conviction for a Tier I violation receives 5 points.

para.text: In addition to any other applicable penalty, a person may be suspended for a period of not less than 180 days and not exceeding 365 days during the oyster harvest season for:

The unlawful harvest of oysters from a leased oyster bottom or from more than 150 feet within an oyster sanctuary, oyster reserve, or area closed to harvest by the Department of the Environment, when the area is designated and marked with buoys or other signage or the person knew or should have known that the harvest of oysters from the area was unlawful; or
A violation of a time restriction for the harvest or possession of oysters by more than 2 hours.

In addition to any other applicable penalty, a person may be suspended for a period of not more than 3 years for an offense related to a violation of the Lacey Act, 16 U.S.C. 3371-3378, including but not limited to conspiracy to commit a violation of the Lacey Act, 18 U.S.C. 371.
Any person who is convicted of an offense under §§D—H of this regulation or similar offenses under federal law in Maryland may:
Have all recreational fishing licenses issued by the Department revoked;
Be prohibited from engaging in any recreational fishing for the period of time described in §§D—H of this regulation;
Be prohibited from applying for any recreational fishing licenses for the period of time described in §§D—H of this regulation;
Not engage in any recreational fishing activities during a resulting period of suspension.
A revoked recreational fishing license is no longer valid for any purpose.

Applying for a recreational fishing license or engaging in recreational fishing activities when prohibited from doing so by this regulation is a misdemeanor and punishable as provided in Natural Resources Article, §4-1201(c), Annotated Code of Maryland.
A person who receives one of the following violations may be suspended for up to 90 days.

para.text: Any person who in any 8 year period receives five suspensions for violations as described in §§E—H of this regulation may be prohibited from engaging in all recreational fishing activities for 5 years.
Any person who in any 3 year period receives three suspensions for violations as described in §D of this regulation may be prohibited from engaging in all recreational fishing activities for 1 year.

Before the Department imposes a suspension or revocation under this regulation, a person is entitled to a contested case hearing upon request in accordance with Natural Resources Article, §4-220, Annotated Code of Maryland.
The Department may agree to a settlement before proceeding to a contested case hearing.

A lesser penalty may be imposed at the Department's discretion if the circumstances warrant it, to include consideration of the person’s disciplinary record, an agreement to comply with any conditions that the Department may reasonably require, and any other mitigating circumstances.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
Balloon Payment.
“Balloon payment” means any scheduled payment on a mortgage loan that is more than two times the average of all other payments scheduled to repay the mortgage loan.
“Balloon payment” does not include a down payment.
“Commissioner” means the Commissioner of Financial Regulation in the Maryland Department of Labor.
“Earned Wage Access” has the meaning stated in Commercial Law Article, §12-1501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Fee” has the meaning stated in Commercial Law Article, §12-1501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Fraud” means any act that:
Constitutes a criminal violation of any article of the Annotated Code of Maryland and is part of an attempt or scheme to defraud any person; or

Whether or not a criminal offense, is an intentional misrepresentation of any material fact or is conduct that is intended to prevent the discovery of a material fact, and is part of an attempt or scheme to defraud any person.
“Initial application” has the meaning stated in COMAR 09.03.06.02B(14).
“Mortgage loan” has the meaning stated in Financial Institutions Article, §11-501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Provider” has the meaning stated in Commercial Law Article, §12-1501, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Regulated person” means an individual, corporation, business trust, financial institution, estate, trust, partnership, association, two or more persons having a joint or common interest, or any other legal or commercial entity that is subject to the regulatory authority of the Commissioner pursuant to Financial Institutions Article, or Commercial Law Article, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Residential real estate” has the meaning stated in Financial Institutions Article, §11-501, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Settlement” means the disbursement of the proceeds of a mortgage loan by the person conducting the settlement to the borrower or to third parties as required by the lender's instructions.
“Tip” has the meaning stated in Commercial Law Article §12-101, Annotated Code of Maryland.
On or before July 1st of each year a Provider of Earned Wage Access shall provide the Commissioner with information including but not limited to the following types:
Total number of consumers served;
Total number of transactions;
Total number of repeat consumers;
Average number of transactions per consumer;
Such consumer demographic information as the Commissioner advises annually;
Default rates for consumers;
Total amount of revenue; and
Total amount of processing fees collected.
The Commissioner shall advise Providers by May 1st of each year of the specific data the Commissioner is seeking.

para.text: Renewal Requirements.

In order to be considered for renewal as a certified general real estate appraiser, certified residential real estate appraiser, licensed real estate appraiser, or appraiser trainee, the applicant shall have completed 14 hours of continuing education for each year or portion thereof the applicant has been certified or licensed, since the initial issuance of the license held or, if appropriate, the most recent renewal. These courses shall be approved by the Commission as set forth in this regulation.
A total of not less than 42 hours of continuing education during each license term is required for renewal.

A licensee or certificate holder shall complete the continuing education required by §A(1)—(2) of this regulation prior to filing any application for renewal of a license or certificate, notwithstanding the expiration date for the license or certificate to be renewed.

Failure to complete the continuing education required by §A(1) — (2) of this regulation in advance of filing any application for renewal of a license or certificate may be considered by the Commission to be a violation of Business Occupations and Professions Article, §16-701(a)(1)(i), Annotated Code of Maryland, among other potential violations related to failure to complete the required continuing education hours.
Required Subject Matter for Continuing Education.
Required Continuing Education in Professional Appraisal Standards, Ethics, Fair Housing, Discrimination, and Bias.

Effective January 1, 2003, all currently licensed appraisers, certified appraisers, and appraiser trainees, at least once every 2 calendar years, shall successfully complete the 7-hour national Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP) update course, or its equivalent as determined by the Appraiser Qualifications Board (AQB) of the Appraisal Foundation.
For licenses and certificates expiring on or after March 1, 2026:

Any currently licensed appraiser, certified appraiser, and appraiser trainee shall complete a 7-hour course the first time they meet the AQB education requirement for Valuation Bias and Fair Housing Laws and Regulations Outline; and

All currently licensed appraisers, certified appraisers, and appraiser trainees applying for license or certificate renewal shall successfully complete during the preceding 3-year license cycle a 4-hour Valuation Bias and Fair Housing Laws and Regulations course.

If obtaining a new credential or upgrading an existing credential individuals shall complete an 8-hour course to meet AQB education requirements in Valuation Bias and Fair Housing Laws and Regulations Outline that includes 7 hours of instruction and a 1-hour exam; and
Every 2 calendar years thereafter, the course length must be at least 4 hours.
The course required by §B(1)(a) of this regulation shall be taken from an instructor who is:
An AQB certified USPAP instructor; and
A State certified residential appraiser or State certified general appraiser.
A classroom hour is 50 minutes out of each 60-minute segment.
The Commission may not approve a continuing education course unless the course:
Is at least 2 hours in duration;
Is taught by an approved educational provider; and
Covers real estate appraisal-related topics, including but not limited to:
Ad valorem taxation;
Arbitration and dispute resolution;
Courses related to real estate appraisal or consulting;
Development cost estimating;
Ethics and standards of professional practice (USPAP);
Land use planning and zoning;
Management, leasing, and timesharing;
Property development and partial interests;
Real estate law, easements, and legal interests;
Real estate litigation, damages, and condemnation;
Real estate financing and investment;
Real estate appraisal related computer applications;
Real estate securities and syndication;
Developing opinions of real property value in appraisals that also include personal property and/or business value;
Seller concessions and impact on value;
Energy efficient items and “green building” appraisals;
Training in racial discrimination; and
Training in implicit bias or appraisal bias.

The Commission may approve up to one half of an individual’s continuing education credit for participation, other than as a student, in appraisal educational processes and programs. Examples of activities for which credit may be granted are teaching, program development, authorship of textbooks, or similar activities which are determined to be equivalent to obtaining continuing education.
The Commission may grant a licensee or certificate holder no more than 8 hours of credit for instruction per day for courses of continuing education.

The Commission may not grant a licensee or certificate holder credit for completion of the same continuing education course offering within the appraiser’s continuing education cycle, with the exception of the 7-Hour National USPAP Update Course, or its AQB-approved equivalent.

para.text: The provider shall submit the request for payment on the form designated by the Department.
The Department reserves the right to return to the provider, before payment, all invoices not properly signed, completed, and accompanied by properly completed forms required by the Department.

Except as provided in §F of this regulation, the Provider shall charge the Program the provider’s customary charge to the general public for similar services. If the service is free to individuals not covered by Medicaid:
The provider:
May charge the Program; and
Shall be reimbursed in accordance with §D of this regulation; and
The provider's reimbursement is not limited to the provider's customary charge.
The Maryland Medical Assistance Program's procedures for payment are contained in the following documents, the provisions of which are incorporated by reference:
The Professional Services Provider Manual and Fee Schedule (Effective January 2025); and
The Medical Laboratories Fee Schedule pursuant to COMAR 10.09.09.07D.
Except as provided in §F of this regulation, the Department will pay for covered services at the lesser of:
Physician's customary charge or acquisition cost unless the service is free to individuals not covered by Medicaid; or
The Department's fees in accordance with §D of this regulation.

The Program shall reimburse providers up to the acquisition cost for diagnostic and therapeutic radiopharmaceuticals, contraceptive products and devices, and injectable medications when the provider submits documentation indicating that the acquisition cost of the product, device, or drug is greater than the reimbursement allowed in §E of this regulation.

The Program reserves the right to negotiate and establish a different fee for a physician or a group of physicians under contract to a hospital to provide services when a portion of the cost of the contract is paid as the hospital's cost, provided this fee does not exceed limitations set forth in §E of this regulation.
Supplemental payments on Medicare claims are made subject to the following provisions:
Deductible insurance will be paid in full;
Beginning with August 1, 2010 dates of service, and subject to the limitations of the State budget, coinsurance, shall be paid:
In full for the following:
Mental health services;
CPT codes that are priced by report;
Claims for anesthesia services;
Claims from a federally qualified health center; and
HCPCS codes beginning with A through W; and
For all other claims, at the lesser of:
100 percent of the coinsurance amount; or
The balance remaining after the Medicare payment is subtracted from the Medicaid rate; and
Services not covered by Medicare are payable according to §E of this regulation.
Payments on Medicare claims are authorized if the:
Provider accepts Medicare assignments;
Medicare makes direct payment to the provider;
Medicare has determined that services were medically justified;
Services are covered by the Program;
Initial billing is made directly to Medicare according to Medicare guidelines.
The provider may not bill the Department or the participant for:
Completion of forms and reports;
Broken or missed appointments;
Professional services rendered by mail; or
Providing a copy of a participant’s medical record when requested by another licensed provider on behalf of the participant.
The Program will make no direct payment to participants.
The Program will reimburse the provider for dispensed drugs at rates established in COMAR 10.09.03. The provider shall bill the Program in accordance with COMAR 10.09.03 using the Pharmacy Invoice.
The Program will reimburse the provider for dispensed medical supplies at actual cost or at rates established by COMAR 10.09.12, whichever is less.
Billing time limitations for claims submitted pursuant to this chapter are set forth in COMAR 10.09.36.
Providers shall bill the Program in the following manner:

A physician whose laboratory is not required to be registered pursuant to Health-General Article, Title 17, Subtitles 2 and 3, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall bill the Program for laboratory services in accordance with procedures required under these regulations;

A physician whose laboratory is registered as a medical laboratory pursuant to Health-General Article, Title 17, Subtitles 2 and 3, Annotated Code of Maryland, shall bill the Program for laboratory services in accordance with procedures required under COMAR 10.09.09.
Reimbursement.

The Program shall reimburse providers for all laboratory and other diagnostic services performed by a physician, or by authorized personnel under that physician's supervision, for that physician's patients.
Reimbursement shall be according to all applicable provisions of COMAR 10.10.06 and fees established according to §D of this regulation.
Maximum reimbursement may not exceed the Medicare laboratory fee established by the Maryland Medicare carrier recognized by the Program.
The Program shall reimburse providers for mental health services performed by a physician according to the fees established under COMAR 10.21.25 and the requirements of this chapter.

para.text: This chapter applies to:
A payor that requires preauthorization or step therapy for health care services; and
A health care provider.
This chapter does not apply to a pharmacy benefits manager that only provides services for:
Workers’ compensation claims pursuant to Labor and Employment Article, Title 9, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Personal injury protection claims pursuant to Insurance Article, Title 19, Annotated Code of Maryland..
In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Carrier” has the meaning stated in Insurance Article, §15-1301, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Commission” means the Maryland Health Care Commission.
“Executive Director” means the Executive Director of the Commission or the Executive Director’s designee.
“Health care provider” has the meaning stated in Health-General Article, §19-108.3, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Health Care Service” has the meaning stated in Insurance Article, §15-10A-01, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Online process” means the process established by carriers that meets the requirements outlined in Health-General Article, §19-108.5(b)(1), Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Payor” means:
A carrier; or;
A pharmacy benefits manager that is registered with the Maryland Insurance Commissioner, except for a pharmacy benefits manager that only provides services for:
Workers’ compensation claims pursuant to Labor and Employment Article, Title 9, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Personal injury protection claims pursuant to Insurance Article, Title 19, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Preauthorization” means the process of obtaining approval from a payor by meeting certain criteria before a certain health care service is rendered by the health care provider.

“Prescriber” means a health care provider who has the required license and, if necessary, scope of practice or delegation agreement that permits the health care provider to prescribe drugs to treat medical conditions or diseases.

“Step therapy or fail-first protocol” is a protocol established by a payor that requires a certain prescription drug or sequence of prescription drugs to be used by an insured individual or an enrollee before another specific prescription drug ordered by a prescriber is covered.
“Supporting medical information” means:
A paid claim from a payor that requires a step therapy or fail-first protocol for an insured or an enrollee;
A pharmacy record that documents that a prescription has been filled and delivered to an insured or enrollee, or to a representative of an insured or enrollee; or
Other information that constitutes sufficient supporting medical information by an insured’s or enrollee’s prescriber and a payor that requires a step therapy or fail-first protocol.
A payor shall establish and maintain online access for a provider to the following:
A list of each health care service that requires preauthorization by the payor; and
Key criteria used by the payor for making a determination on a preauthorization request.
Online Portal for Health Care Services.
A payor shall establish and maintain an online portal that meets the requirements of Health General Article, §19-108.2, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A payor’s online portal established and maintained under this section shall:
Accept electronic preauthorization requests from a health care provider;
Assign a unique electronic identification number to each preauthorization request;
Approve in real time, electronic preauthorization requests:
For which no additional information is needed by the payor to process the preauthorization request;
That meet the payor’s criteria for approval;
Render a determination within 1 business day after receiving all pertinent information on requests not approved in real time of electronic preauthorization requests for pharmaceutical services that:
Are not urgent; and
Do not meet the standards for real-time approval under §B(2)(c) of this regulation; and

Render a determination within 2 business days after receiving all pertinent information of electronic preauthorization requests for health care services, except pharmaceutical services, that are not urgent.
Online Process for Pharmaceutical Services.
A payor shall establish and maintain an online process that meets the requirements of Health General Article, §19-108.5, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A payor’s online process established and maintained under this section shall:

Link directly to all e-prescribing systems and electronic health record systems that use the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs SCRIPT standard and the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs Real-Time Prescription Benefit Standard;
Provide accurate, real-time patient-specific benefit information to insureds, enrollees, and contracted health care providers, including any:
Copayment, deductible, coinsurance, or other out-of-pocket costs;
More affordable medication alternatives; and
Preauthorization requirements;
Provide the information required under this section at the point of prescribing in an accessible and understandable format;
Approve in real-time, electronic preauthorization requests:
For which no additional information is needed by the payor to process the preauthorization request;
For which no clinical review is required; and
That meet the payor’s criteria for approval.
A payor that requires a step therapy or fail-first protocol shall:
Comply with Insurance Article, §15-142(c), Annotated Code of Maryland;
Establish and maintain an online process to allow a prescriber to override the step therapy or fail-first protocol if:
The step therapy drug has not been approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration for the medical condition being treated; or
A prescriber provides supporting medical information to the payor that a prescription drug covered by the payor:
Was ordered by the prescriber for the insured or enrollee within the past 180 days; and
Based on the professional judgment of the prescriber, was effective in treating the insured's or enrollee's disease or medical condition;
Provide notice to prescribers regarding the availability of its online process; and
Provide information to insureds or enrollees on the availability of the step therapy or fail-first protocol within its network.
A payor may not:
Impose a fee or charge a person for accessing its online process established under §C of this regulation; or
Access, without health care provider consent, health care provider data via the online process other than for the insured or enrollee.
If requested by the Commission, a payor shall demonstrate continued compliance with the preauthorization requirements in Regulation .03 of this chapter.

A payor that has been granted a waiver under Regulation .05 of this chapter shall notify the Executive Director if it can no longer demonstrate the extenuating circumstances for which the waiver was granted within 30 days after a payor can no longer demonstrate extenuating circumstances.

A payor may request that the Commission issue or renew a waiver from the requirement to meet a preauthorization requirement in Regulation .03 of this chapter by the demonstration of the following extenuating circumstances:
For an insurer or nonprofit health service plan, a premium volume that is less than $1,000,000 annually in the State;

For a group model health maintenance organization, as defined in Health-General Article, §19-713.6, Annotated Code of Maryland, preauthorization of health care services requested by providers not employed by the group model health maintenance organization; or
Other circumstances determined by the Executive Director to be extenuating.
Submission of Request for Waiver or Renewal of Waiver.
A request for a waiver or renewal of waiver shall be in writing and shall include:
An identification of each preauthorization requirement for which a waiver is requested; and
A detailed explanation of the extenuating circumstances necessitating the waiver.
A request for a waiver shall be filed with the Commission in accordance with the following:
For renewal of a waiver, no later than 30 days prior to its expiration; or

For a payor that becomes authorized to provide services within the State of Maryland after October 1, 2012, within 3 months after the date the payor is authorized to provide benefits or services within the State.
Issuance of Waiver.
The Executive Director may issue a waiver from a preauthorization requirement to a payor that demonstrates extenuating circumstances within this chapter.
The Executive Director will review and provide a decision to approve or deny all waiver requests within a reasonable time frame.
A waiver or renewal of a waiver shall be valid for 2 years, unless withdrawn by the Executive Director.
Withdrawal of Waiver.
The Executive Director may withdraw a waiver or renewal of a waiver if the Executive Director determines that the payor can no longer demonstrate extenuating circumstances.
If the Executive Director withdraws a waiver or renewal of waiver, the Executive Director shall notify the payor, setting forth in writing the reasons for withdrawal.

After a waiver or a renewal of a waiver is withdrawn, a payor shall submit a plan to the Executive Director within 30 days that includes a timeline for attaining each requirement in Regulation .03 of this chapter in a format approved by the Commission.
Review of Denial or Withdrawal of Waiver.

If the Executive Director has denied or withdrawn a waiver, a payor may seek Commission review of the denial or withdrawal by filing a written request for review with the Commission within 20 days of receipt of the Executive Director’s notice of denial or withdrawal of a waiver, which shall:
State with particularity the grounds and factual basis for a payor’s disagreement with the denial or withdrawal decision;
Specify the remedy requested; and
If desired, include a written request to orally address the Commission, which shall be scheduled if requested.

The full Commission may review the written request for review of a denial or withdrawal of a waiver directly or, at the discretion of the Chair of the Commission, appoint a Commissioner to review the request submitted under §E(1) of this regulation, who will make a recommendation to the full Commission.

If a written request was submitted under §E(1)(c) of this regulation, a payor may orally address the Commission before a determination is made by the Commission as to whether or not to issue or withdraw a waiver.

After reviewing and considering a payor’s written request for review of the denial or withdrawal decision and any oral argument, if applicable, the full Commission shall issue a written decision affirming, reversing, or modifying the decision reviewed.
A waiver or renewal of waiver from the requirements of this chapter may not be sold, assigned, leased, or transferred.

A payor that has been granted a waiver under this regulation shall notify the Executive Director if it can no longer demonstrate the extenuating circumstances for which the waiver was granted within 30 days after a payor can no longer demonstrate extenuating circumstances.

On or before July 1, 2026, a health care provider shall ensure that each e-Prescribing system or electronic health record system owned or contracted for by the health care provider to maintain a health record of an insured or enrollee has the ability to access:
The online process established by a payor under Regulation .03C of this chapter; and
The real-time patient out of pocket cost information and available medication alternatives.
Health Care Provider Waiver Requirements.
A health care provider may request that the Commission issue or renew a waiver from the requirements in §A of this regulation by demonstration of the following extenuating circumstances:
Economic hardship;
Technology limitations; or
Other circumstances determined by the Commission to be extenuating.
A health care provider seeking a waiver from the requirements of §A of this regulation shall submit an attestation to the Commission using the form on the Commission’s website.

A payor that becomes authorized to provide services within the State after the effective date of a requirement in this chapter shall meet each requirement within this chapter within 3 months of offering services.
A person that fails to meet a requirement of this chapter may be subject to a financial penalty based on:
The person’s ability to pay;
The willfulness of the improper conduct;
The extent of actual or potential public harm caused by the violation;
The cost of auditing or investigating the matter; and
The person’s history of any previous violation.
A person assessed a fine may challenge the assessment in accordance with the procedure outlined in COMAR 10.25.12.02.

para.text: Examination Eligibility.

An applicant may be approved by the Board to take Exam I upon satisfactory proof that the applicant has graduated, or is expected to graduate at the completion of the immediate semester, from a school or college of pharmacy that has been:
Approved by the Board; or
Accredited by the American Council on Pharmaceutical Education (ACPE).

Beginning May 1, 2026, an applicant may be approved by the Board to take Exam II upon satisfactory proof that the applicant has graduated from, or has successfully completed the didactic portion of the core curriculum of a school or college of pharmacy that has been:
Approved by the Board; or
Accredited by the American Council on Pharmaceutical Education (ACPE).
An applicant may not receive a license until the applicant has:
Obtained passing scores on Exam I, Exam II, and Exam III, as applicable; and

Provided satisfactory proof to the Board of the applicant’s graduation from a college or school of pharmacy approved by the Board or accredited by the American Council on Pharmaceutical Education (ACPE).
Passing Scores—Exam I and Exam II.
The passing score for Exam I is 75.
II Passing Scores.
For the Maryland Multistate Pharmacy Jurisprudence Examination (MPJE) the passing score is 75.
For the Uniform Multistate Pharmacy Jurisprudence Examination (UMPJE) the passing score is 75.
Maryland Pharmacy Law Webinar and Post-Webinar Exam.
The passing score is 85.

The Maryland Pharmacy Law webinar and post-webinar exam shall provide unlimited attempts to pass the exam in one sitting provided that the applicant reviews the webinar after each failed attempt at the exam.
There may not be a fee associated with the Maryland Pharmacy Law webinar and post-webinar exam.

If a candidate receives a passing score on Exam I or Exam II, the candidate is not required to retake that exam unless the passing score was received more than 2 years before the date the candidate successfully completed all of the other examination requirements set forth in this regulation.
Passing Score—Exam III.
Prescreening Test of Oral English Competency.

On completion of a prescreening test application form provided by the Board, an applicant may elect to take a standardized prescreening test of oral English competency given by the Board's agents at times to be determined by the Board.

An agent of the Board possessing post-secondary credentials in an academic discipline acceptable to the Board shall score the prescreening test of oral English competency through the use of a standardized rating system.

An agent of the Board shall design the rating system of the prescreening test so that a passing score demonstrates that an applicant speaks fluent English as proficiently as typical United States native speakers whose English is easily understood by the average pharmacy patient or customer.

By choosing to take the prescreening test of oral English competency, an applicant is certifying to the Board that the applicant speaks English as proficiently as typical United States native speakers whose English is easily understood by the average pharmacy patient or customer.

By choosing to take the prescreening test to verify this certification, the applicant consents to pay the costs of test administration and shall pay the fee indicated on the Board's prescreening application form directly to the Board's agent.
An applicant who passes the prescreening test passes Exam II, the Test of Oral English Competency required under Regulation .02B(2)(b) of this chapter.

An applicant who fails the prescreening test for oral English competency may take the Test of Oral English Competency required under Regulation .02B(2)(b) of this chapter without prejudice to the applicant's licensure application if the applicant pays all related fees.

An applicant who has graduated from a recognized English-speaking professional school accredited by the Accreditation Council for Pharmacy Education is deemed to be proficient in the oral communication of the English language in satisfaction of requirements of the prescreening test.
Test of Oral English Competency.

In order to obtain a license to practice pharmacy, an applicant shall pass a standardized test of oral English competency approved by the Board or the board of pharmacy of another state with a score acceptable to the Board, based upon the Board's standards and the examination taken.
The Board shall provide the applicant with a list of Board-approved testing services and the passing score for each approved test of oral English competency.

Waiver. The Board shall waive the requirement as set forth under §D(2)(a) of this regulation if the applicant graduated from a recognized English-speaking professional school accredited by the Accreditation Council for Pharmacy Education.
Claims of Speech Impairment.
An applicant who claims to have a speech impairment shall submit documentation of the impairment on forms supplied by the Board and completed by a Board-approved:
Licensed physician; and
Speech-language pathologist licensed to practice speech pathology in the United States.
The applicant shall submit the documentation with:
The applicant's initial application; or
Before the applicant's second attempt at passing the test of oral English competency.
After the second examination, the applicant may not submit documentation of impairment unless:
An intervening medical or surgical event caused the impairment;
The applicant claims the impairment before a third examination; and
The applicant provides documentation that is approved by the Board.
The Board may accept documentation of a speech impairment as a passing score on Exam II if the applicant also:

Provides documentation from a Board-approved licensed pharmacist or a Board-approved instructor in a school of pharmacy that the applicant can communicate in a professionally competent manner with patients and health care providers; and

Describes, in a hearing before the Board, the manner in which the applicant would communicate with a licensed prescriber regarding a prescription and would counsel a typical patient on proper drug usage.
The certifying or verifying authority shall send all certificates and verifications directly to the Board for the applicants.
The Board-approved testing services may not accept certificates and verifications sent to or by the applicant.
If any of the documents required under this regulation are in a language other than English, the certifying or verifying authority shall submit a certified translation.
The applicant shall bear the costs of:
Translation by a certified translator;
Taking the test of oral English competency; and
Obtaining documents required under this regulation.
Graduates of a Foreign School or College of Pharmacy.
In addition to the other examination requirements set forth in this regulation, an applicant who has graduated from a foreign school or college of pharmacy shall:
Be approved to take the Foreign Pharmacy Graduate Equivalency Examination (FPGEE); and
Obtain the Foreign Pharmacy Graduate Education Committee (FPGEC) Certificate.
The passing score for the FPGEE is 75.
Upon successful completion of the FPGEE and submission of the FPGE Certificate, an applicant may be approved by the Board to take Exam I and Exam II.

section.heading: Supervisors of Instruction, Assistant Principals, and Principals.

para.text: Application of Regulation.
This regulation applies to obtaining licensure as an Administrator.
If a principal transfers to become a principal in a special education school, the principal shall hold a Supervisor of Special Education license as set forth in Regulation .10 of this chapter.
Administrator Requirements.
This license qualifies an individual to be assigned as a supervisor of instruction, assistant principal, or principal.
The applicant shall have:
A master’s degree, or higher, from an accredited institution of higher education;
Twenty-seven months of effective teaching performance or effective performance as a specialist as defined in COMAR 13A.12.04;
Three semester hours or State-approved CPD credits in special education coursework; and
The applicant shall meet one of the following pathways:
A Department-approved program which leads to licensure as a supervisor of instruction, assistant principal, or principal;
An approved out-of-State program that leads to licensure as a supervisor of instruction, assistant principal, or principal and includes a supervised clinical practicum;
Eighteen semester hours of graduate coursework taken at an institution of higher education, to include coursework in the following categories:
Curriculum, instruction, and assessment;
Supervision, evaluation, and supporting instructional improvement;
Legal issues and ethical decision-making;
Program/school administration;
Culturally responsive leadership and/or equity in leadership; and
Supervised clinical practicum that is a minimum of 240 clock hours; or
Holds a valid professional state license in administration from another state or country and has verification of 5 years of effective performance as an administrator in a PK-12 school setting.

Applicants who qualify based on §3(b) or (c) of this regulation must present a qualifying score as established by the State Superintendent of Schools on a school leader licensure assessment approved by the State Board of Education.
Administrator I and II Endorsements.
The Administrator I and II endorsements are historic.
The Administrator I endorsement:
Qualifies an individual to serve as an assistant principal and supervisor of instruction; and
An individual may continue to hold that endorsement if the requirements for renewal, reinstatement, or both are met.
The Administrator II endorsement:
Qualifies an individual to serve as a principal; and
An individual may continue to hold that endorsement if the requirements for renewal, reinstatement, or both are met.
Definition.
In this regulation, the following term has the meaning indicated.
Term Defined. “Library media administrator” means an individual designated by the local superintendent of schools as having responsibility for the:
Administration and supervision of the library media program, including the supervision of the library media program in the individual schools; and
Development of policies, programs, budgets, and procedures for the library media services of the school system and its schools.
Education and Experience. To be licensed as library media administrator, the applicant shall:
Meet the requirements for licensure as a library media specialist;
Have a master’s degree from an institution of higher education;
Have 3 years of effective library media program experience;
Complete 3 semester hours or State-approved CPD credits in special education coursework; and
Meet the requirements for licensure as an Administrator I or Supervisor of Instruction, Assistant Principals, and Principals under Regulation .05 of this chapter.

Special Provision. An applicant seeking to satisfy the requirements of §B(3) of this regulation may, at the recommendation of the local school superintendent, substitute 2 years of related effective experience for 2 years of library media program experience.
Principal Public Separate School. The requirements for licensure as a principal in a public separate school are that the applicant shall:
Meet the requirements for licensure in special education; and
Meet the requirements for licensure as an Administrator II or Supervisor of Instruction, Assistant Principals, and Principals under Regulation .05 of this chapter.
Supervisor of Special Education (Sole Assignment). The requirements for licensure as a supervisor of special education are that the applicant shall:
Meet the requirements for licensure in special education; and
Meet the requirements for licensure as an Administrator I or Supervisor of Instruction, Assistant Principals, and Principals under Regulation .05 of this chapter.
Special Provision. Supervisors with multiple area assignments shall meet the requirements set forth in Regulation .05 of this chapter.

section.heading: Scope.
section.text: These regulations establish a science-based status assessment protocol to determine the harm and impact caused by invasive plants, including an approval procedure for activities involving invasive plant species that cause or are likely to cause severe harm and administrative orders that the Secretary may issue to enforce these regulations.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.

“Classify as a prohibited invasive plant” means the Secretary is including an invasive plant on the Consolidated List of Maryland Invasive Plant Species in accordance with Agriculture Article, §§9.5-301 and 9.5-301.1, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Commercial entity” means an entity that engages in a plant removal activity for profit.
“Committee” means the Invasive Plants Advisory Committee.

“Consolidated List of Maryland Invasive Plant Species” means a list of plant species classified as prohibited invasive plants in accordance with Agriculture Article, §§9.5-301 and 9.5-301.1, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Early Detection Rapid Response (EDRR) Plant Species” means a nonnative plant species that:
Is not yet widespread in the State but is at risk of:
Becoming established as an invasive species; and
Causing significant damage; and
Has:
Been found in at least one native species habitat in the State; or
Not yet been found in the State.
“Expert Assessor” means:
An individual or a team within the Department or the Department of Natural Resources; or
A qualified independent assessor.
Invasive Plant.

“Invasive plant” means any living part of a plant species or its subspecies that did not evolve in the State and if introduced within the State, will cause or is likely to cause, as determined by the Secretary:
Economic harm;
Ecological harm;
Environmental harm; or
Harm to human health.
“Invasive plant” includes a commercial or noncommercial plant that is terrestrial or aquatic and meets the definition under §B(7)(a) of this regulation.

“Invasive Plant Species Status Assessment Protocol” means a protocol based on the NatureServe’s 2004 protocol (An Invasive Species Assessment Protocol: Evaluating Non-Native Plants for the Impact on Biodiversity. Version 1.)

“Invasiveness Rank” means a rank assigned to a nonnative species to signify its level of invasiveness (i.e., high, medium, low or insignificant) based on the results of an invasive assessment protocol.
“Landscaping services” includes services for ornamental horticultural design, maintenance, and installation of living plants.
“Prohibited invasive plant” includes invasive plant species that cause or are likely to cause severe harm within the State.
“Qualified independent assessor” means an individual or team that:

Possesses at least 2 years of field experience in invasive plant species in Maryland or in nearby jurisdictions, including Washington, D.C., Delaware, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Virginia, and West Virginia; and
Has assessed invasive plant species without supervision from the Department, the Department of Natural Resources or the Committee.
“Watch List” means a list of plant species that:
Have been assessed by an expert assessor in accordance with Agriculture Article, §9.5-301, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Were not determined by the assessment to be eligible for classification as a prohibited invasive plant; and
May be reassessed in the future.

section.heading: Invasive Plant Species Status Assessment Protocol.

para.text: The Secretary shall determine whether an invasive plant qualifies as a prohibited invasive plant or a plant for the Watch List based on the following protocol. An expert assessor shall complete a plant status assessment based on the NatureServe’s 2004 protocol (an invasive species assessment protocol: evaluating non-native plants for their impact on biodiversity. Version 1.)
Assessments completed by an expert assessor shall be reviewed and approved by the Invasive Plant Advisory Committee.

Except as approved by the Secretary under this regulation, a person may not propagate, import, transfer, sell, purchase, transport, or introduce any living part of a prohibited invasive plant in the State.
A person may conduct an activity prohibited under §A of this regulation if:
The person receives approval from the Secretary before conducting the activity; and
The activity is for the purpose of:
Disposing of the invasive plant;
Controlling the invasive plant;
Using the invasive plant for research or educational purposes; or
Exporting the invasive plant out of the State
A person has the Secretary’s approval and may engage in any activity prohibited by §A of this regulation for the limited purpose of:
Controlling a prohibited invasive plant, provided:
The person is not a commercial entity;
The plant is not taken off-site; and
The plant is not placed at a location in the proximate vicinity of a roadway or waterway that could facilitate the movement of the invasive plant off-site; or
Transporting a prohibited invasive plant off-site for the purpose of identification or disposal, provided:
The person is not a commercial entity; and
The plant is removed according to the provisions of Regulation .06G of this chapter.

For approvals not granted under §C of this regulation, a person may submit a written request to the Secretary for approval of any activity allowed under §B of this regulation with the following information:
Name;
Business, nursery, or plant dealer license number if applicable;
Phone;
Email address;
Prohibited invasive plant for which approval is being requested (full botanical name);
Specific location of the prohibited invasive plant;
Proposed activity;
Method of safeguarding dispersal of all propagules and all other living parts of the prohibited invasive plant;
Time frame of the proposed activity; and
Any other information required by the Secretary.
Upon notice and an opportunity to be heard, the Secretary may:
Withdraw or revoke any approval for any person who violates any condition of any approval by the Secretary; or
Revise any approval as a condition of any permit, based on new information obtained after an approval is granted.

The Secretary may issue administrative orders to enforce the provisions of this chapter and Agriculture Article, Title 9.5 (Invasive Plants Prevention and Control), Annotated Code of Maryland, as follows:
Order any person to cease propagating, importing, transferring, selling, purchasing, transporting, or introducing any living part of a prohibited invasive plant in the State;
Order the condemnation and seizure of any prohibited invasive plant owned or possessed by any person who violates Agriculture Article, §9.5-302(b), Annotated Code of Maryland;
Order the marking or tagging in a conspicuous manner of a prohibited invasive plant owned or controlled by any person subject to an order of the Secretary under this chapter;

Order any person, on notice from the Secretary, to dispose of any prohibited invasive plant that is held in violation of this chapter or in violation of Agriculture Article, Title 9.5, Annotated Code of Maryland, in a manner that renders all plant parts nonviable, or allow a person to return a prohibited invasive plant to the out-of-State supplier of the plant, as approved in advance by the Secretary;

Order a person to make any prohibited invasive plant available to the Secretary for destruction if the person has failed to dispose of the prohibited invasive plant as ordered by the Secretary and require that person to pay any destruction cost;
Order a person to take any action that the Secretary considers necessary to enforce the requirements of Agriculture Article, Title 9.5, Annotated Code of Maryland.
A person who violates any order issued by the Secretary is subject to a civil penalty not to exceed $500 for each violation.

section.heading: Prohibited Invasive Plants and Watchlist Plants.

para.text: Prohibited invasive plants are those plants listed on the Consolidated List of Maryland Invasive Plant Species found on the Maryland Department of Agriculture’s website, Plant Protection and Weed Management page.
Phase-out Periods for Prohibited Invasive Plants.

A person in possession of a prohibited invasive plant, except a person who is growing a woody plant in ground, shall comply with the prohibited invasive plant provisions of this chapter within 1 year of that plant’s effective date on the prohibited invasive plant list.
A person who is growing a prohibited invasive plant in ground shall comply with the provisions of this chapter within 2 years from that plant’s effective date on the prohibited invasive plant list.
A person may not acquire a new prohibited invasive plant after that plant’s effective date on the prohibited invasive plant list.

Sales Limited to Existing Stock. During the phase-out period, a person may only sell the existing stock of a prohibited invasive plant that person has in inventory as of the date the plant was placed on the prohibited invasive plant list.
Exemption Request.

The Secretary may consider an exemption request from a nursery, broker or plant dealer licensed under Agriculture Article, §5-309, Annotated Code of Maryland to extend the phase-out period for a specific plant species that has been placed on the prohibited plant list.
In considering an exemption request, the Secretary shall:
Receive and consider input from the Invasive Plant Advisory Committee including the species’ economic harm, ecological harm, environmental harm, and harm to human health; and
Consider the economic impact that the phase-out period has on the affected person.
The person seeking the exemption shall submit a written request to the Secretary providing the same information specified in Regulation .04D of this chapter.
If the Secretary grants a person’s exemption request to extend the phase-out period for a specific plant species, the extension may not be more than 1 year.
The Secretary shall maintain a listing of plants on the Watch List, which listing may be amended from time to time.
Procedures for Classification or Declassification of a Prohibited Invasive Plant and Associated Cultivars.

A person who requests the classification or declassification of a plant as a prohibited invasive plant, shall submit a written request to the Secretary that includes the name of the requestor, contact information, scientific name of the plant, and a justification for the request.
Upon receipt of the request the Secretary, in consultation with the Invasive Plant Advisory Committee, shall make a determination, considering:
Whether the plant is or could potentially be sold or marketed in the State;

If the plant has already been assessed pursuant to Regulation .03 of this chapter, whether new, science-based information is available that could change the species' designation as a prohibited invasive plant; and

If the plant has not been assessed pursuant to Regulation .03 of this chapter, whether the justification for the request, including documented scientific evidence of economic, ecological, or environmental harm, or harm to human health, necessitates formal weed status assessment, assessment of invasiveness, or both.
The Secretary shall notify the requestor of the final decision regarding the request.
Distribution of the Prohibited Invasive Plant List and the Watch List.
The Secretary shall send the Prohibited Invasive Plant List and the Watch List with annual renewal letters and with each new application for a nursery, plant dealer, or plant broker license.
The Secretary shall post the Prohibited Invasive Plant List and the Watch List on the Department's website.
A person in the business of providing landscaping services who plants or supplies for planting a plant on the Watch List shall provide to the customer requesting the service the Watch List.
Disposal of Prohibited Invasive Plants. Any person who removes a prohibited invasive plant off-site shall ensure that:
The plant is completely contained (for example, covered or securely tarped in a solid conveyance, or bagged) to prevent the escape of plant parts into the environment; and
If the plant is being removed for disposal, it is removed to a Maryland Department of Environment approved landfill or compost facility for burial or composting.
